description: src/utils.ts and src/utils/index.ts both re-export the array helpers, so @shirudo/ddd-kit/utils and @shirudo/ddd-kit/utils/array resolve to identical code. Pick one path and drop the other from package.json exports + tsup.config.ts. Also add "sideEffects": false to package.json — none of the modules have top-level side effects, so this is free tree-shaking.
close_reason: Redundant /utils/array subpath removed; sideEffects:false added; pre-existing empty-utils-bundle bug surfaced and fixed by re-exporting helpers directly from src/utils.ts. utils.js: 68B → 7.13KB

title: Migrate Result/Outcome to @shirudo/result, drop internal implementations
description: Internal src/core/result/ (Result + Outcome/Success/Erroneous) duplicates the now-published @shirudo/result package. Adds maintenance surface, two parallel APIs (Audit), and README at L498-505 already imports ok/err/Result from main entry which doesn't compile. Add @shirudo/result as peerDependency, replace all internal imports across aggregate/event-sourced-aggregate.ts, app/{command,command-bus,query-bus}.ts, core/guard.ts, value-object/value-object.ts. Delete src/core/result/, src/result.ts. Remove ./result subpath from package.json exports + tsup.config.ts. Remove Outcome/Success/Erroneous without deprecation window (still RC). Update README.
close_reason: Migrated to @shirudo/result peerDep; removed internal Result/Outcome; tanstack-server-fn examples removed; README + CHANGELOG updated

title: Split aggregate.ts — separate domain-event.ts from aggregate types
description: aggregate.ts mixes Domain Events and Aggregate types. Split into domain-event.ts (DomainEvent, EventMetadata, helpers) and aggregate.ts (Version, AggregateState, AggregateSnapshot, sameVersion, aggregate(), bump()).
close_reason: Created domain-event.ts. aggregate.ts now only has aggregate types + re-exports domain-event.

title: Decouple AggregateEventSourced from AggregateRoot — sibling inheritance from Entity
description:
## Problem

AggregateEventSourced extends AggregateRoot, inheriting setState() and addDomainEvent() which should NOT be used in event-sourced aggregates. State must only change through apply() → handler. The inheritance creates a leaky API.

## Solution

Both AggregateRoot and AggregateEventSourced extend Entity directly. Both implement IAggregateRoot<TId>. Version management duplicated (~30 lines).

```
Entity<TState, TId>
  ├── AggregateRoot<TState, TId>
  └── AggregateEventSourced<TState, TEvent, TId>
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] AggregateEventSourced extends Entity, not AggregateRoot
- [ ] AggregateEventSourced has own version management (private _version, get version(), setVersion())
- [ ] AggregateEventSourced does NOT expose setState(), addDomainEvent()
- [ ] AggregateEventSourced has own snapshot support
- [ ] AggregateRoot unchanged
- [ ] IAggregateRoot implemented by both
- [ ] All tests green
close_reason: AggregateEventSourced extends Entity directly. Own version, pendingEvents, snapshot. No setState/addDomainEvent leakage.

title: IRepository constraint incompatible with AggregateRoot
description:
## Problem

Das Repository Interface in `src/repo/repository.ts:28-33`:

```typescript
export interface IRepository<
  TState,
  TEvent extends DomainEvent<string, unknown>,
  TAgg extends IAggregateRoot<TId> & Aggregate<TState, TEvent>,
  TId extends Id<string>,
>
```

Erfordert `TAgg extends Aggregate<TState, TEvent>`. Das `Aggregate` Interface hat `pendingEvents: ReadonlyArray<Evt>`.

**Problem:** `AggregateRoot` hat `domainEvents` (Getter), **nicht** `pendingEvents`. Nur `AggregateEventSourced` hat `pendingEvents` (als Alias für `domainEvents`).

Das bedeutet: **Das Repository kann strukturell nur mit `AggregateEventSourced` genutzt werden**, nicht mit `AggregateRoot` — obwohl die Docs sagen "Repositories work exclusively with Aggregate Root Entities".

### TypeScript structural typing

```typescript
class Order extends AggregateRoot<OrderState, OrderId> { ... }
// Order hat kein `pendingEvents` → erfüllt `Aggregate<S, E>` NICHT
// → Order kann nicht als TAgg im Repository verwendet werden
```

## Fix

Hängt zusammen mit der Entscheidung zum `Aggregate` Interface (siehe Bead "Aggregate interface missing identity"). Möglichkeiten:

**Option A: Repository-Constraint vereinfachen**
```typescript
export interface IRepository<
  TAgg extends IAggregateRoot<TId>,
  TId extends Id<string>,
> {
```
Entfernt die Abhängigkeit auf `Aggregate`. TState und TEvent werden nicht im Interface selbst benötigt.

**Option B: `pendingEvents` auf `AggregateRoot` hinzufügen**
Als Alias für `domainEvents`. Macht `AggregateRoot` kompatibel mit dem `Aggregate` Interface.

**Option C: Zwei Repository-Interfaces**
Eines für simple Aggregates, eines für Event-Sourced. Over-engineered für ein Toolkit.

## Empfehlung

Option A — das Repository braucht TState und TEvent nicht in seiner Signatur. Es arbeitet mit dem Aggregate als Ganzes.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `AggregateRoot` (ohne Event Sourcing) kann als TAgg im Repository verwendet werden
- [ ] `AggregateEventSourced` funktioniert weiterhin
- [ ] Repository-Interface so simpel wie möglich
- [ ] Alle Tests grün
close_reason: Closed

title: Functional withEvent() does not apply state changes — not event sourcing
close_reason: Closed

title: AggregateRoot.version is public and externally mutable
description:
## Problem

In `src/aggregate/aggregate-root.ts:111`:

```typescript
public version: Version = 0 as Version;
```

Jeder externe Code kann die Version beliebig setzen:

```typescript
order.version = 99 as Version;
```

Das untergräbt Optimistic Concurrency Control komplett. Die Version ist der zentrale Mechanismus, um Concurrent Writes zu erkennen — wenn sie von außen manipulierbar ist, verliert sie ihren Zweck.

Vernon (IDDD, Chapter 10 - Aggregates): *"The version must be controlled exclusively by the aggregate."*

### Betrifft auch

- `AggregateEventSourced` erbt die public version
- `loadFromHistory()` setzt `this.version = history.length as Version` — korrekt als interner Zugriff, aber nur weil es auf der Klasse selbst passiert
- `restoreFromSnapshot()` setzt `this.version = snapshot.version` — gleich

## Fix

```typescript
// aggregate-root.ts
private _version: Version = 0 as Version;

public get version(): Version {
  return this._version;
}
```

Interne Stellen (`bumpVersion()`, `loadFromHistory()`, `restoreFromSnapshot()`) müssen dann `this._version` nutzen.

## Impact

Breaking Change für Code, der `aggregate.version = x` setzt. Lesender Zugriff (`aggregate.version`) bleibt identisch.

`IAggregateRoot` definiert bereits `readonly version: Version` — das Interface ist korrekt, nur die Implementierung ist zu permissiv.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `version` ist `private` mit `public get version()` Getter
- [ ] Kein externer Code kann die Version direkt setzen
- [ ] `IAggregateRoot` Interface bleibt kompatibel (hat bereits `readonly`)
- [ ] `bumpVersion()`, `loadFromHistory()`, `restoreFromSnapshot()`, `restoreFromSnapshotWithEvents()` funktionieren weiterhin
- [ ] Alle Tests grün
close_reason: Closed

title: Aggregate interface missing identity (id)
close_reason: Renamed Aggregate → AggregateState. Removed dead copy entity/aggregate.ts.

title: Add CHANGELOG.md for RC release
description:
## Problem

Es gibt kein `CHANGELOG.md` im Projekt-Root. Für einen Release Candidate und die spätere 1.0.0 ist ein Changelog essentiell:

1. **Breaking Changes**: Consumer müssen nachvollziehen können, welche API-Änderungen zwischen Versionen passiert sind – besonders beim Upgrade von 0.x auf 1.0.
2. **npm-Konvention**: Ein CHANGELOG gehört zum Standard-Set einer veröffentlichten Library (LICENSE ✓, README ✓, CHANGELOG ✗).
3. **Vertrauen**: Ein gepflegtes Changelog signalisiert Stabilität und Professionalität, was bei einer DDD-Toolkit-Library besonders wichtig ist (Enterprise-Kontext).

## Format

[Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/) Format empfohlen:

```markdown
# Changelog

All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.

The format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/),
and this project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html).

## [1.0.0-rc.1] - YYYY-MM-DD

### Added
- ...

### Changed
- ...

### Removed
- ...

## [0.16.0] - YYYY-MM-DD
...
```

## Scope

Das Changelog muss nicht jede 0.x-Version rückwirkend dokumentieren. Mindestens:

- Die geplante RC-Version mit allen Features, Breaking Changes, und Removals
- Optional: eine zusammengefasste "0.x Beta" Sektion als Referenz

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `CHANGELOG.md` im Projekt-Root erstellt
- [ ] Keep a Changelog Format
- [ ] Mindestens RC-Version dokumentiert mit Added/Changed/Removed Sektionen
- [ ] In `files` Array der `package.json` muss CHANGELOG **nicht** aufgenommen werden (wird automatisch von npm inkludiert), aber `.npmignore` darf es nicht excluden
- [ ] Prüfen, dass `.npmignore` CHANGELOG nicht blockiert
close_reason: Closed

title: Disable minification in tsup build config
description:
## Problem

In `tsup.config.ts:15` ist `minify: true` gesetzt. Das ist **falsch für eine Library**:

1. **Debugging**: Minifizierter Code erzeugt unlesbare Stack Traces für Consumer. Auch mit Source Maps ist das Debugging-Erlebnis schlechter, weil nicht alle Tools Source Maps korrekt auflösen.

2. **Bundler-Konflikt**: Consumer nutzen eigene Bundler (Vite, Webpack, esbuild) mit eigener Minification. Doppelte Minification kann zu Problemen führen und verhindert Tree-Shaking-Optimierungen des Consumer-Bundlers.

3. **Konvention**: Etablierte Libraries (zod, drizzle-orm, effect, @trpc/core) liefern alle **unminifiziert** aus. Minification ist Job des Application-Bundlers, nicht der Library.

4. **Bundle-Size-Illusion**: Die minifizierte Library-Größe auf npm sagt nichts über die tatsächliche Größe im Consumer-Bundle aus, da der Consumer-Bundler ohnehin minifiziert.

## Fix

```typescript
// tsup.config.ts
minify: false,  // Libraries should not be minified – that's the consumer's bundler's job
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `minify` in `tsup.config.ts` auf `false` setzen
- [ ] Build ausführen und prüfen, dass `dist/` valide JS/DTS enthält
- [ ] Prüfen, dass die generierten Source Maps korrekt sind
- [ ] Package-Size vor/nach dokumentieren (informativ, kein harter Check)
close_reason: Closed

title: Remove or integrate unused Clock interface from ports.ts
description:
## Problem

In `src/events/ports.ts:82-84` ist das Interface `Clock` definiert:

```typescript
export interface Clock {
  now: () => Date;
}
```

Dieses Interface wird **nirgends** in der Codebase verwendet – weder intern noch in Tests. Es wird aber über `index.ts → ports.ts` öffentlich exportiert und ist damit Teil der Public API.

Für einen RC muss die Public API bewusst kuratiert sein. Ein ungenutztes Interface suggeriert Features, die es nicht gibt, und erzeugt eine Wartungspflicht ohne Nutzen.

## Options

**Option A (empfohlen): Entfernen**
- Clock aus `ports.ts` löschen
- Einfachste Lösung, kein ungenutzter Code in der API

**Option B: Integrieren**
- `createDomainEvent()` in `aggregate.ts` nutzt aktuell `new Date()` direkt
- Clock könnte als optionale Dependency injiziert werden für testbare Timestamps
- Höherer Aufwand, aber bessere Testbarkeit

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `Clock` entweder aus `ports.ts` entfernen ODER in `createDomainEvent` integrieren
- [ ] Wenn entfernt: kein Export von `Clock` mehr in der Public API
- [ ] Wenn integriert: mindestens ein Test, der Clock-Injection zeigt
- [ ] Alle bestehenden Tests grün
- [ ] Build erfolgreich
close_reason: Closed

title: Remove dead code: entity/aggregate-base.ts
description:
## Problem

Die Datei `src/entity/aggregate-base.ts` ist toter Code:

- Sie wird **nicht** aus `src/index.ts` exportiert und ist somit für Consumer unsichtbar.
- `AggregateRoot` (der eigentliche Aggregate-Base-Path) extends `Entity`, **nicht** `AggregateBase`.
- `AggregateConfig` ist in `aggregate-base.ts` **und** `aggregate-root.ts` dupliziert definiert.

Das verursacht Verwirrung bei Contributors und bläht die Codebase unnötig auf.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `src/entity/aggregate-base.ts` entfernen
- [ ] Sicherstellen, dass kein interner Import auf `aggregate-base.ts` zeigt
- [ ] Prüfen, dass `AggregateConfig` nur noch in `aggregate-root.ts` existiert
- [ ] Alle Tests laufen weiterhin grün
- [ ] Build (`pnpm build`) erfolgreich

## Context

Für einen RC darf kein toter Code mitgeschleppt werden, der die öffentliche API-Oberfläche unklar macht. Consumer könnten über die Datei stolpern und denken, es gäbe zwei Wege Aggregates zu bauen.
close_reason: Closed

title: toProblemDetails adopts toProblem hardening or delegates to it
description: src/http/problem-details.ts builds the RFC 9457 body as a mutable Record and casts it, skipping the guarantees base-error 8's toProblem transport stage provides: deep-cloned, deeply frozen, null-prototype body (pollution-proof), enforced JSON-safety with outcome.omitted diagnostics, status validation, and content-type/retry-after headers. The kit is pollution-hardened everywhere else but its own problem body is not. Full delegation would change the wire contract (base-error writes fields: FieldFault[] while the kit writes errors/invalid-params with PublicIssue entries), so either adopt the clone-freeze-JSON-safety hardening locally or delegate and document the wire change. Solving this also subsumes ddd-kit-ts-ffvc (extensions overwriting standard members).
acceptance_criteria: The returned body is deeply frozen with a null prototype and JSON-safe (tests pin all three); extensions can no longer overwrite the RFC 9457 standard members; existing member-key and code assertions stay green; if delegation is chosen, the wire change is documented in the CHANGELOG. Full gates green: pnpm test, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.
close_reason: Converged onto base-error's toProblem pipeline: thin view-literal delegation, ProblemDetailsResult return (status/headers/body/outcome), issues under details.issues symmetric to toPublicErrorView, member option removed, kit-named ValidationProblemResult alias keeps the no-base-error annotation path; parallel hardening deleted before merge; gates green

title: Re-tag v3.0.0 at release so the tag matches the changelog
description: The local tag v3.0.0 points at 23e5e09, which predates the entity prototype-pollution fix now documented under the [3.0.0] section of CHANGELOG.md (per decision on 2026-07-05 the fix is collected under 3.0.0 because the version is not released yet). If the tag is published as-is, the changelog misdescribes the shipped artifact. Fix: before publishing, delete and re-create v3.0.0 on the final commit that includes the fix (and any further pre-release fixes), or move the entry to a 3.0.1 section if 3.0.0 ships from the current tag.
acceptance_criteria: At release time: git tag v3.0.0 resolves to a commit whose tree contains the defineProperty-free spread-based shallowCopyOwned fix and the matching CHANGELOG section, verified via git tag --contains; npm tarball for 3.0.0 contains the fixed dist output.

title: Dependency hygiene: lockfile drift on base-error and vitest critical advisory
description: package.json declares @shirudo/base-error ^8.1.0 as devDependency (peer range ^8.0.0), but pnpm-lock.yaml pins 7.1.1 with specifier ^7.1.1, so the entire test suite runs against a major version consumers are not told to use. Separately, vitest 3.2.4 carries a critical advisory (arbitrary file read/execute via the UI server, patched in 3.2.6; latest is 4.x). Remaining audit findings are all in the docs toolchain (vite/vitepress/typedoc chain) and do not reach dist. Fix: run pnpm install against the declared ranges, commit the lockfile, and bump vitest to at least 3.2.6.
acceptance_criteria: pnpm ls shows @shirudo/base-error 8.x matching package.json; pnpm-lock.yaml specifier matches ^8.1.0; pnpm audit reports no critical advisory on the vitest path; full suite green against the new versions: pnpm test, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.
close_reason: Lockfile synced to declared ranges (base-error 8.1.0, vitest 3.2.6, critical advisory resolved); kit migrated to base-error 8 public-error API (LocalizedPublicError, ProblemDetails with code member); docs and changelog updated; gates green

title: deepOmit drops non-enumerable own string properties, breaking deepEqualExcept
description: src/utils/array/deep-omit.ts:179. deepEqual compares plain objects via Object.getOwnPropertyNames (deep-equal.ts:267, test-pinned), but deepOmit clones from Object.keys only, so both sides of deepEqualExcept lose non-enumerable string props before comparison: objects differing only in such a prop give deepEqual false but deepEqualExcept true. Internally inconsistent too: symbol keys and array custom properties do survive the clone. Fix: use Object.getOwnPropertyNames at line 179, mirroring deep-equal.
acceptance_criteria: Regression tests in deep-omit.test.ts and deep-equal-except.test.ts: two objects differing only in a non-enumerable own string property must give deepEqualExcept false, and deepEqual(deepOmit(x, {}), x) must be true. Tests fail before the fix. Full gates green.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/deep-omit-non-enumerable: getOwnPropertyNames walk with enumerability preservation, deepEqualExcept equivalence restored; tests and gates green

title: utils/array README documents a non-existent import path and an unexported function
description: src/utils/array/README.md lines 8, 30, 78, 122, 158-177 and more. All examples import from @shirudo/ddd-kit/utils/array, but the package.json exports map only defines ., ./utils, ./http, ./presentation, ./testing, so every copied snippet fails with ERR_PACKAGE_PATH_NOT_EXPORTED. The README also documents isBuiltInObject as public API, but it is not exported from any entry and src/utils.ts declares it internal. Fix: rewrite imports to @shirudo/ddd-kit/utils and delete the isBuiltInObject section or actually export the function.
acceptance_criteria: Every import statement in the README resolves against the exports map in package.json (verify by grep against the exports keys, or a node resolve probe in the scratchpad). No documented symbol is missing from the corresponding entry; the api-surface test still pins the surface. Full gates green.
close_reason: README imports point at the real @shirudo/ddd-kit/utils entry (node resolve probe verified); internal isBuiltInObject section removed per the utils.ts policy; bv tooling lock ignored

title: analyzeDomainMachineDefinition bypasses the prepared-definition fast path
description: src/domain-state-machine/analyzer.ts:59-60. The function calls validateDomainMachineDefinition plus copyDomainMachineDefinition directly instead of ensureStableDomainMachineDefinition, so a prepared (already validated, copied, frozen, branded) definition pays full re-validation and deep re-copy on every analysis call. Contradicts the contract documented in definition.ts:151-153; it is the only public pure function that does not recognize the brand. Fix: replace both lines with a single ensureStableDomainMachineDefinition call.
acceptance_criteria: Test in analyzer.test.ts proving the fast path: analyzing a prepared definition must not re-copy (e.g. assert via a Proxy-instrumented raw definition that preparing once and analyzing twice triggers no further property reads, or assert the stable copy is the prepared instance itself). Existing analyzer tests stay green. Full gates green.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/analyzer-prepared-fastpath: analyzer uses ensureStableDomainMachineDefinition; fast path pinned via passthrough spies; gates green

title: AggregateRoot.restoreFromSnapshot lacks the pending-events guard
description: src/aggregate/aggregate-root.ts:318-327. EventSourcedAggregate.restoreFromSnapshotWithEvents throws UnreplayableAggregateError when the target carries unflushed pendingEvents, because harvesting them after markRestored would stamp them with a version baseline they were never part of. AggregateRoot.restoreFromSnapshot performs the same markRestored re-baselining but silently keeps pre-restore pendingEvents, producing events that claim facts from a discarded state lineage. Fix: throw UnreplayableAggregateError when pendingEvents.length is greater than 0 before touching state, mirroring the event-sourced path.
acceptance_criteria: Regression test in aggregate-root.test.ts: record an event, then call restoreFromSnapshot; expect UnreplayableAggregateError and unchanged state. Test fails before the fix. Full gates green.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/restore-snapshot-pending-guard: shared non-overridable guard, per-thrower remediation in UnreplayableAggregateError reasons, clean-snapshot undo recipe documented; two review rounds applied; gates green

title: Unguarded isRetryable call in RetryingTransactionScope masks the original error
description: src/repo/retrying-scope.ts:175. The classifier runs unguarded inside the catch block, so a throwing classifier replaces the original transaction error with no cause attached. Reachable with the default policy: someChainRetryable from base-error throws on circular cause chains. Upstream error-type mapping (e.g. ConcurrencyConflictError to HTTP 409) then silently breaks. Fix: wrap isRetryable in try/catch; on classifier throw treat the error as non-retryable and rethrow the original error, optionally reporting the classifier failure via the observer path.
acceptance_criteria: Regression test in retrying-scope.test.ts: inner scope rejects with an error whose cause chain is circular (err.cause = err) under the default policy; the caller must receive the original error, not a classifier error. Also a test with a custom throwing isRetryable asserting the original error surfaces unchanged. Tests fail before the fix. Full gates green.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/retrying-scope-classifier: classifier guarded like onRetry, original error always surfaces; tests and gates green

title: Bus errorMapper requirement bypassed by string-literal-union error channels
description: src/app/command-bus.ts:60-62 and src/app/query-bus.ts:60-62. The conditional type [E] extends [string] is true for every subtype of string, e.g. E = DB_CONN | TIMEOUT literal unions, so options stay optional and the constructor silently installs describeThrown cast to E. A thrown error is then delivered as an arbitrary string outside the declared union, falling through exhaustive switches. Contradicts the documented contract that a typed channel never falls back to string values. Fix: flip the conditional to [string] extends [E] in both bus files.
acceptance_criteria: Compile-time regression in bus-typed-errors.test.ts: with E as a string literal union, constructing the bus without errorMapper must be a type error (ts-expect-error pins it); with E = string it stays optional. pnpm typecheck and pnpm test green.
close_reason: Fixed on refactor/bus-shared-internals: shared bus-internals module, strict two-directional BusArgs gate, ErrorMapperFailedError containment; review findings applied; gates green

title: v3(repo): consolidate repository delete on delete(aggregate)
description:
Maintainer decision (2026-07-04 interview): unify the delete contract on the aggregate-taking variant. Today IRepository.delete(id) (pure row removal, no harvest) and IUnitOfWorkRepository.delete(aggregate) (deletion-event harvest, identity-map tombstone, deleted-cannot-be-resaved gate) coexist.

v3: IRepository.delete takes the aggregate; design decisions inside this bead: (a) whether IUnitOfWorkRepository collapses into IRepository (its remaining delta is only exists()), (b) guidance for withCommit-only setups without an identity map (the docs deletion paths 1-3 in repository.md still hold), (c) contract-suite updates. Migration: callsites load the aggregate instead of passing a bare id; the GC-style bulk cleanup case gets a documented escape (a repository-specific method, not the port).
acceptance_criteria: One delete contract; repository guide and contract suites updated; migration note.
close_reason: One delete contract: IRepository.delete(aggregate), IUnitOfWorkRepository is now the documented minimal subset (delta only exists()). Design decisions recorded: no interface inheritance (generic Evt mismatch not worth the coupling), withCommit-only flow documented (aggregates + deleted arrays), id-only bulk cleanup explicitly excluded from the port (repository-specific purge methods). Type-level RED via ts-expect-error; contract suites unchanged (already aggregate-shaped); repository guide reworked (pattern 2 now uses the deleted marker, pattern 3 load-then-delete + bulk escape). CHANGELOG Changed (breaking) with migration. 983/983.

title: v3(exports): remove computeBackoffDelay, hard-rename Key/PathSegment
description:
Breaking export cleanup for the v3 window (follow-up to ddd-kit-ts-uu7l; the API-surface test turns both into explicit diffs):
1. Remove computeBackoffDelay from the public surface (marked @internal since 2.x; move its unit tests to direct source imports).
2. Hard-rename the generic root-namespace types from deepOmit: Key to DeepOmitKey, PathSegment to DeepOmitPathSegment. Maintainer decision (2026-07-04 interview): NO 2.x deprecated aliases, rename lands in v3 only, with a CHANGELOG migration note.
keep freezeShallow: documented as a standalone consumer utility in 2.2.
acceptance_criteria: Surface test updated intentionally; migration notes in CHANGELOG; typedoc clean.
close_reason: computeBackoffDelay removed from the package entry (module export stays for its direct-source unit tests); Key/PathSegment hard-renamed to DeepOmitKey/DeepOmitPathSegment per maintainer decision (no aliases). RED via the api-surface guard; d.ts surface diff verified to be exactly the intended three changes. CHANGELOG Removed (breaking) with migration note. 983/983.

title: v3(release): ship 3.0.0 with migration guide
description: Closes the v3 window: consolidate the CHANGELOG breaking entries into one migration guide section (per-change before/after snippets), review engines (current Node LTS) and peer-dep majors (@shirudo/base-error, @shirudo/result) so all breaking rides one window, bump to 3.0.0, tag, GitHub release, npm publish (OTP by maintainer). Release posture: no RCs.
acceptance_criteria: 3.0.0 on npm; migration guide in CHANGELOG; all v3 beads closed.
notes: Reopened 2026-07-05: registry check after the reported publish shows versions ending at 2.2.0 and dist-tag latest=2.2.0; 3.0.0 is not on npm. Everything else (tag, GitHub release, CHANGELOG, version bump) is done; only the publish is outstanding.

title: v3.0.0: tightening major (breaking window)
description:
The v3 breaking window, deliberately small and mechanical: every change fails at COMPILE time instead of changing runtime semantics silently, each with a CHANGELOG migration note. Decided in the 2026-07-04 v3 interview (maintainer): implement soon on main via normal PRs in quick succession, then release 3.0.0; if a 2.x hotfix is needed meanwhile, branch from the v2.2.0 tag.

Scope (children of this epic):
1. setState bumpVersion becomes required, autoVersionBump removed (ddd-kit-ts-u9to)
2. buses throw UnregisteredHandlerError from execute (ddd-kit-ts-efo8)
3. export cleanup: remove computeBackoffDelay, hard-rename Key/PathSegment (no 2.x aliases per maintainer decision)
4. consolidate repository delete on delete(aggregate)
5. release task: migration guide, engines/peer-dep review, version 3.0.0

Explicitly OUT of scope (decided): deepFreezeState default flip (perf), commit() rename (design-decisions.md), per-event commitSequence (additive minor when needed, see ddd-kit-ts-yyg7).
close_reason: v3 epic complete: all five work packages shipped (u9to, efo8, cryw, 8455, zyqs) across PRs 10-12; released as v3.0.0 on 2026-07-05. Out-of-scope decisions honored (no deepFreezeState default flip, commit() name kept, commitSequence parked as needs-driven minor in ddd-kit-ts-yyg7).

title: v3(buses): execute throws UnregisteredHandlerError instead of returning err
description:
Follow-up to ddd-kit-ts-bwzi (2.x shipped the named UnregisteredHandlerError delivered through the error channel for compatibility). In v3, CommandBus.execute and QueryBus.execute THROW UnregisteredHandlerError for unregistered types instead of returning err(errorMapper(...)), completing the crash-loud wiring-bug posture (MissingHandlerError, EventHarvestError precedent): a mis-wired bus should fail the request loudly, not surface as an expected failure a generic err-branch can absorb.

Scope: both buses; update the CQRS guide section "Unregistered handlers are wiring bugs" and the UnregisteredHandlerError JSDoc; CHANGELOG migration note (catch-and-map guidance for consumers that relied on the err channel); executeUnsafe already throws.
acceptance_criteria: v3: execute throws; docs and migration notes updated; tests adjusted.
close_reason: v3: execute throws UnregisteredHandlerError on both buses; errorMapper only sees failures a registered handler produced (pinned: mapper not called). JSDoc, CQRS guide and CHANGELOG migration notes updated. TDD, 980/980.

title: v3(aggregate): make the bumpVersion argument on AggregateRoot.setState required
description:
Follow-up to ddd-kit-ts-clsn (decision recorded there; deprecation shipped in 2.x). In v3, change AggregateRoot.setState(newState, bumpVersion?) to setState(newState, bumpVersion: boolean) so every mutation states its OCC intent explicitly at the call site. Rationale: the implicit default (autoVersionBump=false) permits silent lost updates (save writes WHERE version = v SET version = v; a concurrent writer that loaded the same v commits over it without ConcurrencyConflictError). A required argument is superior to flipping the default because upgrading code fails to COMPILE instead of silently changing version arithmetic.

Scope: AggregateRoot.setState signature; decide the fate of AggregateConfig.autoVersionBump (likely remove: with a required arg the config fallback is dead); CHANGELOG migration notes; update aggregates guide and the deprecation notices shipped in 2.x (JSDoc on setState and autoVersionBump).
acceptance_criteria: v3: bumpVersion required at compile time; autoVersionBump decision recorded; migration path in CHANGELOG; guides updated.
close_reason: v3: bumpVersion is a required argument on AggregateRoot.setState (never-overload keeps Entity base compatibility, TypeError runtime guard fires before mutation for compiler-bypassing calls); autoVersionBump removed, AggregateConfig is now an EntityConfig alias. TDD (type-level RED via unused ts-expect-error, runtime guard pinned), guides + CHANGELOG migration notes. 980/980.

title: design(entity): nested state is mutable through the public state getter
description:
Why: Entity.state returns the internal state shallow-frozen. Nested objects stay mutable from OUTSIDE the aggregate: order.state.items.push(x) mutates aggregate internals, bypasses validateState, bypasses the version bump, AND is invisible to changedKeys (the top-level reference did not change, so a partial-write repository never persists it). The trade-off is documented (performance, Tell-Dont-Ask), but it is the single biggest aggregate-invariant gap in the kit (Evans EE-05, Wirfs-Brock RW-13).

Fix options (evaluate, pick one, document in design-decisions.md):
(a) Dev-mode deep freeze: deep-freeze state in development (NODE_ENV !== production or an explicit flag), keep shallow freeze in production. Violations surface as TypeError in dev/test with zero prod cost.
(b) Opt-in config: AggregateConfig.deepFreezeState for consumers who accept the cost.
(c) Rename the raw accessor to make the escape explicit (state stays a projection-friendly read, raw access via a loudly named member). Breaking, v3.
Recommendation: (a) now, evaluate (c) for v3.
acceptance_criteria: Decision recorded and implemented; docs updated; if (a): tests prove nested mutation throws in dev-mode and prod behavior is unchanged.
close_reason: Decision: opt-in deepFreezeState on EntityConfig/AggregateConfig (option b, explicit config instead of NODE_ENV magic: the kit is edge-runtime-first and env detection is unreliable there). Implemented across constructor/setState/apply/snapshot-restore paths via a protected freezeState method, TDD (8 tests), documented as plain-data-only with in-place ownership transfer of nested inputs. Default stays shallow.

title: feat(outbox): specify getPending ordering and add failure/dead-letter affordances
description:
Why (two related port gaps, Hohpe GH-13 plus ordering):
1. Ordering: withCommit promises subscribers per-aggregate causal order ("subscribers MUST process them in that order"), but the Outbox port does not require getPending to return records in insertion/commit order; the contract suite itself notes a SELECT without ORDER BY returns rows unordered. A dispatcher built naively on the port can violate the promised causal order.
2. Failure path: the port knows only add/getPending/markDispatched. No attempt count, no markFailed, no dead-letter concept: a poison message is redelivered forever at the port-contract level.

Fix:
1. Document an ordering requirement on getPending (insertion order per the add() call sequence; recommend a monotonic position column) and add the guarantee to what RepositoryContractEnvironment.committedOutboxEvents / dispatcher docs assume.
2. Add failure affordances: either extend Outbox or add an optional DispatchTrackingOutbox extension interface with markFailed(dispatchId, error) and attempt metadata on OutboxRecord; document the retry-then-dead-letter dispatcher pattern in the outbox guide. Update InMemoryOutbox as the reference implementation.
acceptance_criteria: Port JSDoc specifies ordering; failure/DLQ affordance exists (or an extension interface); InMemoryOutbox and the outbox guide updated.
close_reason: getPending ordering (commit order) is now part of the Outbox port contract with SQL guidance (monotonic position + ORDER BY), pinned by test on InMemoryOutbox. New DispatchTrackingOutbox extension (markFailed + deadLetters, attempts on OutboxRecord); InMemoryOutbox implements it with maxDeliveryAttempts (default 5), dead-lettered records leave getPending, markDispatched clears them too; outbox guide documents the retry-then-dead-letter dispatcher loop. TDD, 962/962.

title: fix(es-aggregate): loadFromHistory on a non-fresh aggregate desyncs persistedVersion
description:
Why: the loadFromHistory JSDoc blesses loading history onto an aggregate that is already at v=1 from a pending creation event ("an aggregate already at v=1 (e.g. after a creation event) loading 2 events ends at v=3"). In that case markRestored(startVersion + N) sets persistedVersion=3 although the pending creation event was never persisted and the stream holds only N events: repositories then route UPDATE (or append with a wrong expectedVersion) against a row/stream that does not match, producing a false ConcurrencyConflictError, and the harvest would stamp the creation event with a version the stream does not carry.

Fix: restrict loadFromHistory to fresh aggregates: throw (a BaseError-derived programming-bug error, crash-loud posture like MissingHandlerError) when pendingEvents.length > 0, or when version > 0 while persistedVersion === undefined. Remove the blessing example from the JSDoc; document that reconstitution happens on bare instances created by the repository. Consider the same guard for restoreFromSnapshotWithEvents.
acceptance_criteria: loadFromHistory rejects non-fresh aggregates with a descriptive error; JSDoc example corrected; tests pin the guard; CHANGELOG notes the tightening.
close_reason: loadFromHistory and restoreFromSnapshotWithEvents now guard the replay target (new UnreplayableAggregateError, crash-loud BaseError posture); the blessing JSDoc example removed, guide corrected; catch-up on persisted aggregates stays supported and is now pinned by tests. TDD, 927/927.

title: feat(aggregate): add a schema version to AggregateSnapshot
description:
Why: AggregateSnapshot carries state, version and snapshotAt but no schema version (Greg Young GY-11). When the TSnapshotState shape evolves, a store has no field to detect, migrate or discard old snapshots; the break surfaces as a runtime failure on the first method call after a much later restore.

Fix: add an optional readonly schemaVersion to AggregateSnapshot; add a protected snapshotSchemaVersion (default 1) on BaseAggregate that createSnapshot stamps; restoreFromSnapshot and restoreFromSnapshotWithEvents compare it and by default reject a mismatch with a descriptive error, with an overridable migrateSnapshotState(stored, storedSchemaVersion) hook for consumers who migrate instead of discarding. Document discard-and-refold-from-stream as the default strategy for ES aggregates. While touching the type: make the AggregateSnapshot fields readonly.
acceptance_criteria: Snapshots carry schemaVersion; mismatch handling (reject or migrate hook) implemented and tested; fields readonly; guide updated.
close_reason: AggregateSnapshot carries schemaVersion (stamped from overridable snapshotSchemaVersion, default 1); both restore paths check before assignment: mismatch throws the new SnapshotSchemaMismatchError (InfrastructureError, discard-and-refold guidance) or routes through the overridable migrateSnapshotState hook; legacy snapshots without the field are treated as schema 1 (pinned by test); fields readonly. TDD, 962/962.

title: feat(es): ship an EventStore port and an event-sourced repository contract suite
description:
Why: the event-sourced pathway is under-served relative to the state-stored one. State-stored gets ports, UnitOfWork integration and the repository contract suite; ES gets aggregate-side machinery only: no EventStore port (no append(streamId, events, { expectedVersion })), no readStream, and no contract tests proving stream-append OCC, replay equality, or snapshot round-trips. The README advertises event sourcing as a first-class feature (Greg Young GY-05: blind append is a lost-update bug waiting to happen).

Fix: 
1. Define an EventStore<Evt> driven port: append(streamId, events, options with expectedVersion) returning a conflict-typed failure (reuse ConcurrencyConflictError), readStream(streamId, fromVersion), and optionally a SnapshotStore port.
2. Extend @shirudo/ddd-kit/testing with createEventSourcedRepositoryContractTests: mandatory two-writer append conflict on expectedVersion, replay equality (fold of stream equals saved state), snapshot+events-after restore, append idempotency posture, rollback purity.
3. Alternatively (if out of scope): scope the README claim down to "aggregate-side event sourcing support" so the promise matches the shipped guarantees.
acceptance_criteria: EventStore port shipped with JSDoc contract and mapped error semantics, plus an ES contract suite; or the README/docs claim is explicitly narrowed.
close_reason: Shipped: EventStore<Evt> port (expectedVersion-guarded atomic append, readStream with fromVersion), InMemoryEventStore reference impl (9 TDD tests), and createEsRepositoryContractTests in the testing entry (7 suite tests: mandatory two-writer append conflict, replay equality/fold order, null-for-unknown-stream, identity map, rollback purity + retried first save, fromVersion slicing, capability-gated duplicate-create). Shared contract assertions extracted for both suites; in-repo reference adapter with mutant pinning (blind append, reversed read). Guide section, README bullet, CHANGELOG. 950/950.

title: fix(aggregate): setState default skips the OCC version bump (silent lost updates)
description:
Why: AggregateConfig.autoVersionBump defaults to false and setState(newState) falls back to it, so a domain method using plain setState produces version-less mutations. Concurrency hole: writer A saves WHERE version=5 SET version=5 (no bump); writer B, which loaded v5 concurrently, then commits WHERE version=5 and silently overwrites A. No ConcurrencyConflictError is ever raised: lost update by default. commit() is safe (always bumps) but setState is the documented mutation primitive on Entity/AggregateRoot.

Fix proposal: make the safe thing the default.
- v3 (breaking): make the bumpVersion argument required on AggregateRoot.setState, or flip autoVersionBump default to true (opt OUT for cosmetic caches).
- v2.x (non-breaking): document the lost-update scenario prominently in the aggregates guide and the setState JSDoc; consider a dev-mode console warning when setState is called without an explicit bumpVersion and no autoVersionBump config was given.
The contract-suite mutateVersionOnly capability already covers the persistence side; this issue is about the in-memory default.
acceptance_criteria: Decision recorded; v2.x docs updated with the lost-update scenario; v3 change (required arg or default true) scheduled in the CHANGELOG migration notes.
close_reason: Decision: 2.x ships deprecation of the implicit default (JSDoc on setState/autoVersionBump with lost-update mechanics, aggregates-guide warning box, CHANGELOG Deprecated entry); v3 makes bumpVersion a required argument (compile-time forcing beats a silent default flip). v3 work split into follow-up bead.

title: Post-commit observers leak async rejections (onPersistError/onPublishError)
description: withCommit's post-commit observers are typed (...) => void, but a void return admits an async function. An async observer whose promise rejects escaped the synchronous try/catch and surfaced as an unhandledRejection AFTER a committed write (process crash under Node default policy), violating the post-commit invariant the feature promises. Found by /code-review on the onPersistError change; onPublishError had the same latent bug. Fix: a shared reportToObserver() helper neutralises both a synchronous throw and an async rejection for both observers. Regression test proven to fail without the fix.
acceptance_criteria: An async, rejecting onPersistError/onPublishError does not produce an unhandled rejection and does not reject the committed write; regression test present and proven to fail without the fix.
close_reason: Fixed via reportToObserver helper for both observers; regression test proven to fail without fix; 909 tests + typecheck + build green

title: withCommit swallows post-commit markPersisted/onPersisted failures with no observer
description: handler.ts post-commit loop catches all errors from markPersisted, the user-overridable onPersisted hook, and clearPendingEvents with a bare (documented) catch and NO observation channel, asymmetric with bus.publish which has onPublishError. A failing user onPersisted hook after commit vanishes with zero signal (Majors CM-14/CM-13). Add a symmetric onPersistError(error, aggregate) observer to withCommit deps and UnitOfWorkDeps; observer-only (its own throw is swallowed), never rejects the committed write. Found via architecture-review audit.
acceptance_criteria: withCommit and UnitOfWork accept optional onPersistError; it fires with (error, aggregate) when markPersisted/onPersisted/clearPendingEvents throws; a throwing observer does not break the post-commit invariant; the committed write still resolves; tests cover it; CHANGELOG updated.
close_reason: Implemented onPersistError observer + extracted classifyRunError; tests + typecheck + 908 tests + build green

title: deepEqual ignores non-enumerable string properties (false positive)
description: comparePlainObjects used Object.keys (enumerable strings only) while counting all symbols via getOwnPropertySymbols, so two objects differing only by a non-enumerable string property compared equal. Fix: use Object.getOwnPropertyNames for string keys, consistent with the symbol handling. Found via code-review workflow.
acceptance_criteria: deepEqual detects a differing non-enumerable string property; objects with identical hidden properties still compare equal; regression test covers it.
close_reason: Fixed with regression tests; typecheck + 904 tests + build green

title: deepEqual compares accessor-defined array elements by getter identity, not value
description: deep-equal.ts array branch compared array elements defined as accessor (getter) properties by get/set function identity, so two arrays whose index getters yield the same value were reported unequal. Inconsistent with comparePlainObjects, which reads accessor properties by invoked value. Fix: read array elements by access (invoking getters); sparse holes stay distinguished via the own-key set comparison. Found via code-review workflow.
acceptance_criteria: deepEqual compares getter-defined array elements by yielded value; sparse hole vs explicit undefined still distinguished; regression tests cover both.
close_reason: Fixed with regression tests; typecheck + 904 tests + build green

title: cloneForVo keeps non-enumerable symbol keys, breaking ValueObject clone-equals-original
description: cloneForVo's plain-object walk skips non-enumerable STRING keys but keeps non-enumerable SYMBOL keys (line 309 guards on typeof key === 'string'). ValueObject.clone() rebuilds via { ...this.props } spread, which drops the non-enumerable symbol, while deepEqual counts all symbols, so x.equals(x.clone()) === false, breaking the clone-equals-original invariant. Fix: skip all non-enumerable own keys (string and symbol) in cloneForVo so props only carry the enumerable value surface that survives a spread. Found via code-review workflow.
acceptance_criteria: cloneForVo drops non-enumerable symbol keys like it drops non-enumerable string keys; x.equals(x.clone()) holds for a VO whose input had a non-enumerable symbol; regression test covers it.
close_reason: Fixed with regression tests; typecheck + 900 tests + build green

title: Evaluate and implement a domain machine definition analyzer
description: Create a side-effect-free development/test utility that inspects the declarative transition graph for unreachable states, structural dead ends, terminal reachability, and a reviewable transition matrix. Guard-dependent runtime reachability must not be presented as statically provable.
acceptance_criteria: A written design decision defines which diagnostics are sound; analyzer never executes guards, reducers, initialContext, or snapshot validators; diagnostics are deterministic and typed; adversarial definitions and graph edge cases are tested; utility is omitted if evaluation shows insufficient value, with the decision documented.
close_reason: Implemented a typed, deterministic, side-effect-free definition analyzer with sound graph diagnostics, frozen transition matrices, adversarial tests, and documented proof boundaries.

title: Document production DDD patterns for machine inputs
description: Document snapshot evolution and migration boundaries, duplicate-message and idempotency strategies for process managers, and deadlines as explicit domain inputs rather than runtime timers. Keep inbox/outbox, clocks, persistence, and retries outside the domain machine.
acceptance_criteria: Guide includes concrete examples for versioned persisted snapshots, inbox deduplication or idempotent transitions, and deadline inputs carrying explicit time; examples preserve output versus Domain Event separation; no timer, persistence, retry, or EventBus behavior is added to the core machine.
close_reason: Documented versioned snapshot migration, transactional inbox/outbox deduplication with optimistic concurrency, explicit deadline inputs, and output versus Domain Event boundaries.

title: Clarify DDD value versus XState
description: Explain in the DomainStateMachine versus XState section that XState can model the same lifecycle and the kit machine exists for its stricter enforced DDD contracts.
close_reason: Clarified that XState can model the same lifecycles and that the kit machine's value is its narrower DDD contract and runtime boundaries.

title: Turn XState comparison into decision table
description: Replace the prose DomainStateMachine versus XState section with a concise comparison table and decision rule.
close_reason: Replaced the prose comparison with a verified capability and fit table plus concise decision rule.

title: Document DomainStateMachine versus XState
description: Add a short decision guide after the State vs context section explaining when to use the kit machine and when XState is the better fit.
close_reason: Added and verified the DomainStateMachine versus XState decision guide.

title: Preserve deepOmit array length descriptor
description: deepOmit skips the array length descriptor and changes writable:false to writable:true despite descriptor-preservation claims. Reapply it after cloning entries and add regression coverage.
close_reason: deepOmit now restores the complete Array length descriptor after cloning entries; covered non-writable length.

title: Document forbidden state machine transitions
description: Clarify in the Domain State Machine guide how to model forbidden transitions: omit impossible transitions, use guards for conditional transitions, and terminal states for end states.
close_reason: Documented how to model forbidden, guarded, and terminal domain state machine transitions

title: Harden DomainStateMachine API before release
description: Defensively copy/freeze snapshots and outputs, prevent wrapper behavior from changing when callers mutate the original definition, and document the ownership contract.
close_reason: Hardened DomainStateMachine snapshots, outputs, and wrapper definition ownership

title: Add DomainStateMachine building block
description: Implement a small domain-state-machine API for finite named domain states with typed context, guards, reducers, outputs, snapshot validation, tests, and documentation.
close_reason: Implemented DomainStateMachine API with tests and documentation

title: Convert swap-prone error constructors to options objects; remove redundant createDomainEventWithMetadata
description: Breaking-release cleanup. AggregateNotFoundError(aggregateType,id,cause?), DuplicateAggregateError(aggregateType,aggregateId,cause?), and ConcurrencyConflictError(aggregateType,aggregateId,expectedVersion,actualVersion,cause?) have adjacent same-typed positional params (two strings; two strings + two numbers) that can be silently swapped at the consumer's repository call site. Convert to a single options object. Remove createDomainEventWithMetadata: it is a redundant wrapper over createDomainEvent(type,payload,{metadata}) and has two trailing object params (metadata,options) that are swappable. Convention to follow going forward: positional for 1-2 distinct params, options object for 3+ / multiple optionals / adjacent same-typed params. Fold into the in-flight 2.0 breaking release.
close_reason: Converted AggregateNotFound/Duplicate/ConcurrencyConflict ctors to options objects; removed redundant createDomainEventWithMetadata. All call sites/tests/docs/CHANGELOG updated. 711 tests green.

title: HTTP: Problem Details mapping for the kit's HTTP-semantic errors
description: Use base-error v7.1's defineProblemDetailsAdapter (and the structural PublicErrorView) to pre-configure an RFC 9457 mapping for the kit's known errors: AggregateNotFoundError->404, ConcurrencyConflictError->409, DuplicateAggregateError->409, ValidationError->422, fallback->500. Ships from the opt-in @shirudo/ddd-kit/http entry. The kit knows its errors' transport semantics; consumers stop hand-wiring status codes. Safe by default: title is a static per-type summary (no occurrence data / id leak), technical message and details are never emitted. Additive, opt-in. Candidate (1) from the base-error v7 follow-up evaluation.
notes: Pivoted from the HTTP status catalog (candidate 1) to Option B after the headless review: shipping per-error HTTP status (404/409/422) is a transport opinion that base-error deliberately leaves to the consumer, so it does not belong in a headless kit. Shipped instead: transport-neutral toPublicErrorView(error) in the new opt-in @shirudo/ddd-kit/presentation entry. It returns base-error's PublicErrorView (code/message/locale/details); NO status/header/exit-code. Consumer feeds it into defineProblemDetailsAdapter (HTTP), gRPC, or CLI. Safe-by-default messages, total over unknown, ValidationError issues via publicIssues(). This is also the opt-in home for the client-safe messages 2.0 removed from the core.
close_reason: Shipped toPublicErrorView in @shirudo/ddd-kit/presentation (Option B, headless: transport stays with the consumer). 5 new tests, gates green.

title: UoW doc: mark the repository-contract test suite as MANDATORY, not recommended
description: The whole exactly-once guarantee is null unless every repository honors the enrollment + identity-map contract, and the shipped @shirudo/ddd-kit/testing contract test suite is what pins it. Currently it reads as recommended. In docs/guide/unit-of-work.md (and the repository guide), state plainly that running the contract test suite against every repository implementation is required for the UoW guarantees to hold, with a short why. Cheap, high-value.
close_reason: Done on feat/uow-abortsignal: contract-suite-mandatory danger callout + cancellation section + scope-sig sync + 3 correctness fixes (uer); AbortSignal pre-flight/context/scope-forward + 6 tests (0e9)

title: UoW exactly-once harvest is a repository contract, not structural: a repo that forgets enrollSaved silently drops events
description: The exactly-once event harvest + markPersisted guarantee rests entirely on every repository calling get-before-hydrate, enrollSaved before write, and enrollDeleted. A hand-written repo that forgets enrollSaved drops that aggregate's events with NO error (they simply never harvest). This replaces the old withCommit footgun (forgot to list aggregate) with forgot-to-enroll: better (once per repo, pinned by the contract test suite) but still a silent failure mode. Investigate a structural mitigation: e.g. a dev-mode assertion that detects writes-without-enrollment, or a repository base that enrolls automatically. Strongly mitigated today by @shirudo/ddd-kit/testing contract tests IF consumers run them (see the doc bead).
close_reason: UnenrolledChangesError end-of-run guard via precise load-vs-commit pending-count delta in IdentityMap; best-effort net (contract suite = full mitigation); code-review cleanups (private iteration, shared duck-type helper, best-effort doc); 6 tests

title: UnitOfWork.run(): thread an AbortSignal/timeout through to the TransactionScope
description: run() holds the transaction open for the full duration of the work callback with no cooperative cancellation. A slow saga step pins a DB connection indefinitely; the kit delegates time-bounding entirely to the scope/driver. Add an options arg (run(work, { signal })) that propagates an AbortSignal (and/or a timeout) into TransactionScope.transactional so callers can bound a unit of work. Biggest production-robustness gap from the 1.2.0 UoW review.
close_reason: Done on feat/uow-abortsignal: contract-suite-mandatory danger callout + cancellation section + scope-sig sync + 3 correctness fixes (uer); AbortSignal pre-flight/context/scope-forward + 6 tests (0e9)

title: feat(outbox): aggregateVersion as a first-class field — distinct from event schema version
description:
Checklist review 2026-06-11: DomainEvent.version is the SCHEMA version (defaults 1, for upcasting); the AGGREGATE version is not carried on events or outbox records at all. OutboxRecord is currently { dispatchId, event }. Consumers need aggregateVersion for ordering, idempotency, debugging, replay, and external projections — and the two versions MUST remain distinct fields (never overload one).

Design questions to settle: (a) where it lives — on EventMetadata (stamped by recordEvent? the aggregate knows its version at record time, pre-bump vs post-bump must be pinned — proposal: the version AFTER the mutation that recorded the event, i.e. the value the row will be committed with) vs stamped by withCommit at harvest (then all events of one aggregate share the commit version, simpler, but per-event causality ordering inside one commit is lost); (b) OutboxRecord shape — flattening { id, aggregateId, aggregateType, aggregateVersion, eventType, eventSchemaVersion, payload, occurredAt } is a BREAKING change for every custom Outbox implementer; an additive optional field on the event metadata is non-breaking. Since 1.1.0 is released, prefer the additive path unless a major is planned.

The kit is pre-stable on the UoW feature (Unreleased) but Outbox shipped in 1.x — decide additive-vs-breaking explicitly. Cross-link: projections guide uses last_event_id idempotency; aggregateVersion would strengthen that story.
close_reason: aggregateVersion shipped as optional DomainEvent field, distinct from event.version (schema). Decision: stamped by withCommit at harvest onto frozen copies (commit version = OCC row version; pre-set values via CreateDomainEventOptions win; pendingEvents untouched; Outbox port unchanged — fully additive). Per-commit granularity documented (ES stream positions stay the event store's concern). Contract suite mandatory test asserts committed outbox events carry it; outbox guide recommends indexed columns. 4 new withCommit tests.

title: feat(uow): Phase 1 — explicit-save UnitOfWork core (run API, RepositoryRegistry, lifecycle errors, nesting guard, post-commit marking)
description:
Implement the explicit-save UnitOfWork as a wrapper over withCommit (NOT beside it — outbox handoff, post-commit markPersisted, and best-effort bus publish are inherited). Scope per requirements doc §4/§5/§14/§15/§19/§21-Phase-1:
- UnitOfWork<TContext>.run(work) where the context exposes tx-bound repositories (RepositoryRegistry) + the raw transaction handle. Registry shape per decision bead (enrollment model).
- Context invalidation after close: repository/transaction use after commit or rollback throws TransactionClosedError (§5.4).
- Nesting: forbid in v1 — NestedUnitOfWorkError when run() is called while a UoW is active on the same UnitOfWork instance (§14 Option A; join semantics later).
- Error taxonomy: TransactionClosedError, NestedUnitOfWorkError, CommitError, RollbackError as InfrastructureError subclasses; ConcurrencyConflictError passes through UNCHANGED (§9). Dual-failure: if callback AND rollback fail, throw the primary error with the rollback error attached as cause/secondary (§15).
- Post-commit marking via enrolled aggregates (per decision bead), reusing withCommit's loop semantics (per-aggregate isolation of onPersisted failures).
- Explicit repo.save() only; NO auto-flush, NO savepoints, NO distributed tx (§21).
Tests: requirements doc §23 transaction + post-commit + concurrency + repository-context suites. Docs: new guide section 'Unit of Work' cross-linking withCommit, repository.md partial-writes, and the revised design decision.
design:
API surface fixed by the three decision beads (9or/90w/efe, all decided 2026-06-11):
- UnitOfWork<TCtx>.run(work) wraps withCommit; context = { repositories: <typed registry from user-supplied factory map (tx, session) => repo>, transaction: TCtx }.
- Enrollment: repository registration via UnitOfWorkSession.enrollSaved/enrollDeleted (see ddd-kit-ts-90w design).
- Repo contract: new IUnitOfWorkRepository with delete(aggregate); IRepository untouched (see ddd-kit-ts-efe design).
- design-decisions.md section rewritten ('TransactionScope stays minimal; the Unit of Work lives above it') — keep docs consistent with it.
- Context invalidation scope (be honest in docs + acceptance criteria): enforceable only for what the kit controls — registry getters and session.enroll* throw TransactionClosedError after close; a user holding a raw repo/tx reference past close is a documented contract violation, not a runtime guarantee.
notes:
MikroORM blueprints for this phase (see epic notes for the full analysis):
- Nesting/concurrency guard: #working flag; nested run() inside an active UoW throws NestedUnitOfWorkError (MikroORM: ValidationError.cannotCommit via AsyncLocalStorage insideFlush, uow.ts ~593-617). MikroORM additionally QUEUES concurrent top-level flushes — for v1 we throw on both nested AND concurrent run() on the same instance; a queue can come later if a real use case appears.
- Deterministic close: postCommitCleanup() pattern in a finally block (~1155-1170) — clear enrolled-aggregate set, invalidate the registry/context (subsequent repo/tx use throws TransactionClosedError), reset the active flag. Runs on BOTH commit and rollback paths.
- Single-use semantics: deliberately diverge from MikroORM (EM-scoped, reusable after failed flush) — our run() context is per-operation and dead after close, matching concurrency.md's operation-scoped-aggregates rule.
close_reason: Explicit-save UnitOfWork shipped: run() facade over withCommit, RepositoryFactories registry, UnitOfWorkSession enrollment (saved/deleted, idempotent, save-after-delete throws), error taxonomy (NestedUnitOfWorkError/TransactionClosedError/CommitError/RollbackError/AggregateDeletedError), context invalidation after close, 20 tests, guide + LLM.md + CHANGELOG. Design note: nesting guard is per-instance single-flight; instances are per-operation (parallel-safe by construction).

title: feat(uow): Phase 2 — per-UoW IdentityMap (type+id key, repo integration, deleted-aggregate guard)
description: Ship an IdentityMap implementation (requirements doc §10/§16/§20/§21-Phase-2): get/set/delete/has keyed on aggregateType + id (Restaurant:123 and Booking:123 are distinct entries; key includes the type, never the id alone). Repositories check the map before hydrating and register after loading, so two get(id) calls in one UoW return the SAME instance — this turns the documented Identity Map contract (repository.md, currently the implementer's job) into shipped infrastructure and makes withCommit's reference-dedupe sound by construction. Deleted-aggregate handling: delete(aggregate) marks the entry deleted; a later save() of the same aggregate in the same UoW throws; map cleared when the UoW closes. Tests: §23 identity-map suite. Generic over TId extends Id<string> (no string-typed ids).
notes: MikroORM IdentityMap.ts is the reference implementation (~154 lines, two-level design): Map<AggregateClass, Map<idHash, aggregate>> with lazily created per-type stores (getStore, ~62-73) — cleaner than string-concatenated 'Type:id' keys and gives free per-type iteration. delete() must clean up ALL keys for the instance; MikroORM tracks alternate (business-key) hashes per entity in a WeakMap (#alternateKeys, ~23-53) so cleanup never leaks — adopt the WeakMap pattern if/when findOne-style business-key caching is added (NOT v1). Deleted gate: stricter than MikroORM — re-saving a deleted aggregate in the same UoW THROWS instead of being silently ignored (~518-525 there).
close_reason: IdentityMap shipped: class-keyed two-level storage, strict set (no-op same instance, throws on hydrated-twice violation), deletion tombstones (AggregateDeletedError on re-registration), per-run lifetime with clear-on-close, session.identityMap with TransactionClosedError guard. 13 new tests (9 standalone + 4 UoW integration), guide section, repository.md tip, LLM.md, CHANGELOG.

title: decide(uow): repository surface for UoW — delete(aggregate) vs delete(id), keep Id<Tag> branding
description: Requirements doc §16/§20 wants UnitOfWorkRepository with get(id: string), save(aggregate), delete(aggregate). Two conflicts with the existing kit: (1) IRepository.delete(id) takes an id — the UoW needs the AGGREGATE for event harvest + identity-map removal + deleted-cannot-be-resaved (§16); decide overload vs new interface vs adapter. (2) get(id: string) would lose the Id<Tag> brand — the UoW interfaces MUST stay generic over TId extends Id<string>. Also decide how the existing deletion patterns (repository.md: hard-delete with event harvest via delete(id) inside withCommit) map onto the new surface. Blocks Phase 1.
design:
DECIDED (2026-06-11):
1. IRepository.delete(id) stays UNCHANGED — it serves Pattern-3 hard deletes (GC, no aggregate in hand) and existing consumers.
2. NEW separate interface for UoW-facing repos (name TBD in Phase 1, working name IUnitOfWorkRepository<TAgg extends IAggregateRoot<TId>, TId extends Id<string>>): getById(id: TId), getByIdOrFail(id: TId), save(agg: TAgg), delete(agg: TAgg). delete takes the AGGREGATE because the UoW needs it for (a) pending-event harvest of recorded deletion events, (b) identity-map removal, (c) the deleted-cannot-be-resaved gate; the row deletion itself uses aggregate.id internally.
3. Branded ids EVERYWHERE: TId extends Id<string>; the requirements doc's get(id: string) is rejected — string-typed ids would erase the kit's brand discipline.
4. Concrete repos may implement both interfaces. The documented hard-delete-with-event pattern maps 1:1: order.recordDeletion(reason) then uowRepo.delete(order) — enrollment + harvest replace the manual aggregates-array bookkeeping.
close_reason: Decided and recorded in design fields; Phase 1 (ddd-kit-ts-rdt) design updated with the resulting API surface

title: decide(uow): aggregate enrollment — repository registration vs callback return
description: withCommit gets its aggregates from the callback's RETURN VALUE ({ result, aggregates: [...] }) — a known footgun: forget to return an aggregate and its events never reach the outbox, silently. The requirements doc (§19) has repositories REGISTER saved aggregates with the UoW instead; with a RepositoryRegistry + IdentityMap this falls out naturally and is structurally safer (save() enrolls; impossible to forget). Decide: (a) repo-registration as the UoW model with withCommit's return-array kept for standalone use; (b) how delete() enrolls (events harvested, aggregate marked deleted in identity map); (c) whether read-only loads enroll (no — only save/delete). The decision shapes the UnitOfWorkRepository interface, so it blocks Phase 1.
design:
DECIDED (2026-06-11): REPOSITORY REGISTRATION.
- The UoW hands repository factories a session handle alongside the tx: UnitOfWorkSession { enrollSaved(agg): void; enrollDeleted(agg): void }.
- Repos call enrollSaved(aggregate) at the end of save(); delete(aggregate) calls enrollDeleted(aggregate). Enrollment is idempotent per instance (Set semantics, mirrors withCommit's reference dedupe).
- run() passes the enrolled set to withCommit as its aggregates array — deleted aggregates included, so their recorded deletion events are harvested into the outbox; the post-commit markPersisted on a deleted instance is harmless (caller discards the reference, same as today's documented Pattern 2).
- Read paths do NOT enroll (Phase 2: they only register in the identity map).
- RATIONALE: the footgun moves from per-call-site (every use case must remember the aggregates array) to per-implementation (each repo calls enroll once, verified once by that repo's tests). MikroORM uses the same model (persist/remove stacks fed by API calls, not by callback returns).
- withCommit's return-array path stays fully supported for standalone (non-UoW) use.
notes: MikroORM reference: separate #persistStack / #removeStack Sets keyed by instance (uow.ts ~47-50) with remove() evicting from the persist stack (~518-525) — the registration-based model this decision leans toward is exactly MikroORM's, minus cascades (aggregates have no relation metadata; the aggregate boundary IS the cascade boundary). Repos' save()/delete() enroll instances into UoW-internal stacks; the UoW never attaches tracking state to the aggregate itself.
close_reason: Decided and recorded in design fields; Phase 1 (ddd-kit-ts-rdt) design updated with the resulting API surface

title: decide(uow): revise the 'no Fowler UoW' stance in design-decisions.md
description: docs/guide/design-decisions.md currently documents 'TransactionScope (not Fowler UoW: no change tracking)' as a deliberate decision. The UoW epic (ddd-kit-ts-nnr) builds exactly a Fowler-style UoW in phases — legitimate evolution now that AggregateRoot has dirty tracking (changedKeys/hasChanges, shipped 2026-06-11), but the documented decision must be consciously revised, not silently contradicted. Deliverable: rewrite the design-decisions.md entry explaining WHY the stance changed (dirty tracking made the textbook full-aggregate repository affordable; the UoW facade removes the enrollment footgun) and what stays true (TransactionScope remains the low-level port; withCommit remains usable standalone).
close_reason: design-decisions.md section rewritten: TransactionScope stays minimal, change detection on the aggregate, orchestration in withCommit, opt-in UoW facade as planned next layer

title: epic(uow): UnitOfWork facade over withCommit — explicit-save UoW with repository registry, identity map, error taxonomy
description:
Build a UnitOfWork layer per the requirements doc (2026-06-11 session). KEY INSIGHT FROM GAP ANALYSIS: a large part is ALREADY SHIPPED — withCommit + TransactionScope cover transaction lifecycle (§5), post-commit aggregate lifecycle (§7: markPersisted only after commit, never on rollback, events cleared only at markPersisted), OCC propagation (§9: ConcurrencyConflictError distinguishable, retryable), outbox-in-same-tx + post-commit publish (§13), and the multi-table save pattern with changedKeys/hasChanges dirty tracking (§8/§18, built + review-hardened 2026-06-11: hasChanges covers version-only changes, pendingEvents, keyless states). The UoW MUST be built as a wrapper over withCommit, not beside it — requirements-doc Phase 3 (outbox) is therefore already done.

GENUINELY NEW: (1) UnitOfWork<TContext>.run() facade handing the callback tx-bound repositories via a RepositoryRegistry (§4/§20) instead of the raw tx; (2) shipped IdentityMap implementation keyed type+id (§10); (3) nesting guard NestedUnitOfWorkError (§14, Option A: forbid first, join later); (4) error taxonomy TransactionClosedError/CommitError/RollbackError + dual-failure handling preserving the primary error, context invalidation after close (§5.4/§15); (5) delete(aggregate) semantics + deleted-cannot-be-resaved (§16); (6) optional read-only mode (§17); (7) optional auto-flush LAST (§12/§24: explicit save first).

Blocked by three design decisions (separate beads) and sequenced in phase beads. Acceptance criteria: requirements doc §22; required tests: §23.
notes:
INSPIRATION (user-provided, battle-tested): MikroORM UnitOfWork — github.com/mikro-orm/mikro-orm/blob/master/packages/core/src/unit-of-work/UnitOfWork.ts (analyzed 2026-06-11, ~1725 lines, plus ChangeSet.ts + IdentityMap.ts).

ADOPT (fits the kit's philosophy):
1. Double-flush guard: #working flag; concurrent commit() calls QUEUE and run sequentially; a flush triggered INSIDE a flush hook throws (ValidationError.cannotCommit, uow.ts ~593-617). Direct blueprint for NestedUnitOfWorkError + the run()-active guard.
2. postCommitCleanup() in a finally block (~1155-1170): clears all stacks + working flag deterministically — blueprint for our context-invalidation-after-close.
3. Two-level IdentityMap: Map<EntityClass, Map<idHash, entity>> with per-type lazy stores (IdentityMap.ts ~6-73) — exactly our type+id keying. Alternate-key hashes (business-key lookups) tracked in a WeakMap for cleanup — optional later, note for Phase 2.
4. Separate persist/remove stacks with a one-way deleted gate: remove() drops the entity from the persist stack; a managed+removed entity is never resurrected (~518-589). We go STRICTER: re-saving a deleted aggregate THROWS (explicit > MikroORM's silent ignore).
5. UoW-internal tracking state in Maps/Sets keyed by instance (never hidden __props on the tracked object) — matches our frozen-state aggregates.

REJECT (does not fit):
- Proxy/metadata-driven dirty tracking → our aggregate-level changedKeys/hasChanges shallow reference diff is the kit's answer.
- Auto-flush before queries (~386-401) → explicit save, v1 has no auto-flush at all.
- Metadata-driven cascades + automatic inverse-relation cleanup → aggregates own their consistency boundary; hand-written repos, no relation metadata.
- Hidden wrapper props (__managed/__originalEntityData) on entities → frozen plain-record states forbid it anyway.
- Payload-mutating flush hooks → withCommit's harvest is our only 'onFlush'; events are deeply frozen.

CONCURRENCY DIFFERENCE (keep ours): MikroORM checks the version pre-flush against the in-memory snapshot (lockOptimistic ~1264-1288); the kit checks at the DB row (UPDATE ... WHERE version = persistedVersion) — stronger, actual-DB-state check. No change.

SCOPE DIFFERENCE: MikroORM's UoW is EntityManager-scoped and reusable after a failed flush (stacks survive). Ours is run()-scoped and single-use (TransactionClosedError after close) — simpler and matches the operation-scoped-aggregates rule in concurrency.md.

CHILDREN
  ↳ ✓ ddd-kit-ts-syz: feat(uow): Phase 2 — per-UoW IdentityMap (type+id key, repo integration, deleted-aggregate guard) ● P2
  ↳ ○ ddd-kit-ts-las: decide(repo): DuplicateAggregateError / constraint-violation mapping ● P3
  ↳ ✓ ddd-kit-ts-rdt: feat(uow): Phase 1 — explicit-save UnitOfWork core (run API, RepositoryRegistry, lifecycle errors, nesting guard, post-commit marking) ● P2
  ↳ ✓ ddd-kit-ts-efe: decide(uow): repository surface for UoW — delete(aggregate) vs delete(id), keep Id<Tag> branding ● P2
  ↳ ○ ddd-kit-ts-7am: feat(outbox): aggregateVersion as a first-class field — distinct from event schema version ● P2
  ↳ ○ ddd-kit-ts-nc8: feat(uow): Phase 4 (optional) — auto-flush ● P4
  ↳ ✓ ddd-kit-ts-90w: decide(uow): aggregate enrollment — repository registration vs callback return ● P2
  ↳ ○ ddd-kit-ts-hox: feat(testing): Repository Contract Test Suite — RELEASE BLOCKER for any stable/public UoW claim ● P1
  ↳ ○ ddd-kit-ts-kc5: feat(uow): Phase 3 (optional) — read-only UoW mode ● P3
  ↳ ✓ ddd-kit-ts-9or: decide(uow): revise the 'no Fowler UoW' stance in design-decisions.md ● P2
  ◐ 5/10 complete (50%)

RELEASE GATE (checklist review 2026-06-11, user decision): the UoW/OCC feature must NOT be claimed stable/public until the Repository Contract Test Suite (ddd-kit-ts-hox, P1) ships — OCC is a repository CONTRACT the kit must make testable, not a documented pattern. Hard acceptance phrasing: 'Die UoW-Boundary erfüllt die Mindestdefinition strukturell. Die OCC-Garantie ist aber nur erfüllt, wenn jedes Consumer-Repository das Version-Predicate korrekt implementiert und die Contract-Tests besteht.'

title: feat(aggregate): dirty-tracking on AggregateRoot — changedKeys() since restore (unit-of-work write support)
description:
Repositories for aggregates whose state spans multiple tables (root row + N child-collection tables) currently have two bad options on save: write everything (write amplification), or have the application service orchestrate per-collection writes manually — which reopens the door to forgotten OCC version bumps and per-step boilerplate.

MOTIVATING ANTI-PATTERN (observed in a real consumer codebase): an aggregate keeps its child collections OUT of aggregate state because a full-aggregate save couldn't know which collections changed. Consequences cascade: collection writes bypass the aggregate, the OCC version no longer bumps by construction, and a domain-level "touch" method exists solely to advance the version manually — opt-in per application-service method, silently forgettable (a forgotten call compiles, passes single-writer tests, and saves successfully while staying invisible to every other instance's OCC check; the consumer had to add a hand-written assertion in its save path to catch it). Library-level dirty tracking dissolves that whole workaround and makes the textbook full-aggregate repository affordable.

WHY IT'S CHEAP IN DDD-KIT (unlike Hibernate-style proxy/deep-diff dirty checking): setState() replaces state immutably and _state is freezeShallow'd. Unchanged top-level sub-objects therefore KEEP REFERENCE IDENTITY across mutations — a shallow per-key !== comparison between the current state and the state at restore time is an EXACT dirty check, O(top-level keys), no proxies, no deep diff, no setState bookkeeping.

Anatomy fit (current src/aggregate/*): BaseAggregate owns version/persistedVersion/markRestored/markPersisted but no state; Entity owns _state + freezeShallow; AggregateRoot joins both — so AggregateRoot is the layer that can snapshot the state reference at the persistence-lifecycle markers and diff lazily on demand.

Scope: AggregateRoot only. EventSourcedAggregate doesn't need it — its pendingEvents ARE the change record.
close_reason: Implemented changedKeys/hasChanges dirty tracking on AggregateRoot with docs and 23 new tests; all gates green

title: assertSnapshotSafe: spoofed-tag bypass and symbol-key false negative
description: src/aggregate/base-aggregate.ts:394/409 — (a) a plain object with Symbol.toStringTag='Date' matches SNAPSHOT_SAFE_TAGS and is skipped, so nested functions escape the fail-fast walk and die later as pathless DataCloneError (fix: brand-verify via isBuiltInObject); (b) symbol-keyed state passes the guard but structuredClone silently drops it (fix: flag symbol keys as snapshot-unsafe); (c) Reflect.ownKeys also walks non-enumerable props structuredClone ignores (stricter than reality, fails closed — acceptable but document). Both VERIFIED. Found by code review of fix branch.
close_reason: Fixed: brand-verified guard, Error + symbol-key fail-fast, structuredClone-aligned walk; 5 new tests

title: shadowMutators crashes on sealed/non-extensible (not frozen) Date
description: src/value-object/value-object.ts:50 — guard checks Object.isFrozen only; a sealed Date with a writable expando is not frozen but non-extensible, so Object.defineProperty(obj,'setTime',...) throws 'object is not extensible'. Regression vs main (deepFreeze handled sealed Dates fine). VERIFIED. Fix: guard on !Object.isExtensible(obj). Found by code review of fix branch.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/p1-review-fixes; covered by 12 new tests

title: SNAPSHOT_SAFE_TAGS whitelists Error: subclasses silently downgraded in snapshots
description: src/aggregate/base-aggregate.ts:324 — custom Error subclasses in aggregate state pass the class-instance check ([object Error] tag) and structuredClone silently downgrades them to plain Error (instanceof broken, custom fields partially lost) — the exact silent corruption assertSnapshotSafe exists to catch. VERIFIED. Decide: treat Error in state as snapshot-unsafe (fail fast) or document. Found by code review of fix branch.
close_reason: Fixed: brand-verified guard, Error + symbol-key fail-fast, structuredClone-aligned walk; 5 new tests

title: vo() silently drops symbol-keyed properties; existing test passes vacuously
description: src/value-object/value-object.ts:62 — structuredClone does not clone symbol keys, so vo({ [Symbol('tag')]: { nested: 1 } }) silently loses the property. The existing test vo.test.ts:508-516 ('freezes properties whose key is a Symbol') passes vacuously — v[tag] is undefined and Object.isFrozen(undefined) === true. The deepFreeze docstring's claimed symbol-key symmetry with deepEqual is unreachable through vo(). Either preserve symbol keys or document+throw.
close_reason: Fixed: cloneForVo walks plain structures preserving symbol keys; vacuous test made strict; 4 tests

title: deepFreeze immutability guarantee broken for Date/Map/Set internals
description: src/value-object/value-object.ts:42 and src/aggregate/domain-event.ts:406 — Object.freeze does not protect internal slots: event.occurredAt.setTime(0) succeeds on a frozen event, and vo({ m: new Map() }).m.set(...) succeeds on a frozen VO.
